coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive and biocompatible Tyrosinase sensor based on one-step synthesis of carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, T.
Chen, Y.
Wang, Y.
coredata.dc:description: Melanoma poses a great threat to human health. However, the typical methods of early diagnosis and real-time detection are still challenged due to low sensitivity and complicated operations. Tyrosinase (TYR) is a key biomarker in melanoma development. Currently, few methods based on rapid real-time detection of TYR are available. Rapid detection methods using carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent probes are emerging. Here, we developed an optimal synthetic route for the preparation of DA-CDs that showed a good response to TYR in water, cell lysates, and mice serum samples. The generation of our DA-CDs did not require crosslinker and other adjuvants. It only required appropriate proportions of citric acid (CA) and dopamine (DA), and 330 s of microwave-assisted heating. Our design greatly shortened the synthetic time of DA-CDs and applies it to the rapid and sensitive detection of TYR for the first time. The linearity response for TYR was within the concentration range from 0.2 U/mL to 5 U/mL. In addition, the synthetic DA-CDs exhibited good biosafety in vitro and in vivo. Therefore, our DA-CDs have the potential to be mass-produced for TYR detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probe
Biosensor
Melanoma
Early diagnosis
Microwaved-assisted method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties, stability, and biosafety research
Selective response of TYR and mechanism
Determination of TYR activity in actual samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Graduate Research and Innovation Projects of Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province ( KYCX21-0145 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of DA-CDs and detection of TYR.
(a) TEM image of DA-CDs. Inset: particle diameter distribution histogram. (b) AFM image. Z scale: 4 nm. (c) HRTEM image. (d) FT-IR spectra of CD + DA mixture and DA-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of DA-CDs under neutral condition. (b) Linear plots of fluorescence quantum yield (QY) for fluorescein (λex = 490 nm, λem = 520 nm) and DA-CDs (λex = 320 nm, λem = 450 nm). (c) Fluorescence intensity of DA-CDs in PBS buffer at various pH.

(a) The cell viability of HepG2 incubated with or without DA-CDs (40 μg/mL) for 24 h, 48 h, and 72 h. (b) Clone formation assays of HepG2. HepG2 (200/well) incubated with or without DA-CDs (40 μg/mL) for 10 days until colonies were clearly visible (>50 cells). (c) Quantitative clone formation assays under a light microscope. (d) H&E-stained sections of major organs.

Selectivity investigation of the DA-CDs-based sensing system for TYR. The concentration of Leu, Trp, Cys, Hcy, NAC, GSH, GLU, urea, and H2O2 were 50 μM; BSA, IgG, ACP, ALP, HRP, GOX, LZM, EST, and PKA were 5 μg/mL. FBS was used at 10 times dilution. TYR was 5 U/mL. All mixture incubated with DA-CDs (40 μg/mL) for 10 min, and then fluorescence responses were obtained under 320 nm excitation.

(a) Visualization and (b) fluorescence spectra of the DA-CDs solution after reacting with different concentrations of TYR (0–20 U/mL) for 30 min. (c) A linear relationship between (F0–F)/F0 and TYR concentration.

(a) Determination of TYR in tap water samples. (b) The levels of TYR in NIH-3T3, HepG2, and B16F10 cell lysates. (c) Photographs and (d) H&E-stained sections. (e) The levels of TYR of the lungs on days 0, 5 and 10 after tumor inoculation.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive and biocompatible Tyrosinase sensor based on one-step synthesis of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Melanoma poses a great threat to human health. However, the typical methods of early diagnosis and real-time detection are still challenged due to low sensitivity and complicated operations. Tyrosinase (TYR) is a key biomarker in melanoma development. Currently, few methods based on rapid real-time detection of TYR are available. Rapid detection methods using carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent probes are emerging. Here, we developed an optimal synthetic route for the preparation of DA-CDs that showed a good response to TYR in water, cell lysates, and mice serum samples. The generation of our DA-CDs did not require crosslinker and other adjuvants. It only required appropriate proportions of citric acid (CA) and dopamine (DA), and 330 s of microwave-assisted heating. Our design greatly shortened the synthetic time of DA-CDs and applies it to the rapid and sensitive detection of TYR for the first time. The linearity response for TYR was within the concentration range from 0.2 U/mL to 5 U/mL. In addition, the synthetic DA-CDs exhibited good biosafety in vitro and in vivo. Therefore, our DA-CDs have the potential to be mass-produced for TYR detection.

Tyrosinase (TYR) is regarded as a standard biomarker in the diagnosis of melanoma. DA-CDs with good biosafety was derived from citric acid and dopamine by one-step microwave-assisted method within 330 s, which showed a good response to TYR in water, different cell lines, and the serum of pulmonary metastatic melanoma mice. The sensing system based on DA-CDs has promising applications for early diagnosis and real-time detection of TYR activity in patients with melanoma.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Melanoma is the fifth most common cancer among adults, and its incidence has been rising worldwide [1,2]. Melanoma can arise from pigment-producing cells throughout the body but most commonly arises in the skin due to UV injury [3]. Histopathology and dermatoscopy are currently the main primary clinical method for melanoma [4,5]. However, because some melanomas are featureless and hidden at an early stage, early diagnosis of melanoma is almost always a challenging task [4]. Diagnostic limitations lead to a much higher risk of death for patients with melanoma. Benefiting from modern bioanalytical techniques, new methods for early detection of cancers are rapidly developing, including biomarker identification and tracking, spectroscopic measurements, ultrasonic testing, and statistical data analysis [6–9].

Tyrosinase (TYR) is a polyphenol oxidase involved in the melanogenesis pathway. It exquisitely catalyzes the oxidation of o-diphenol (l-DOPA) to o-diquinone, which is then converted to melanin [10]. Overexpression of TYR is a predisposing factor for melanoma, which leads to the malignant transformation of melanocytes into melanoma cells [11–13]. Therefore, TYR activity is regarded as a standard biomarker in the diagnosis of melanoma [14]. To date, many studies have confirmed the importance of TYR in the clinical diagnosis of melanoma [15–17].

Traditional approaches to the determination of TYR activity include colorimetric, electrochemical, photoelectrochemical, and Raman spectroscopic analysis [18–21]. Fluorescent probe-based TYR activity detection is becoming preferred over conventional assays because it offers the prospect of accurate diagnosis through real-time, noninvasive, and high-resolution imaging [22–24]. For example, Cui et al. recently report a coumarin-based two-photon (TP) fluorescent off-on probe for detecting TYR activity [25]. The probe was designed by connecting coumarin to 3-hydroxylbenzyl (a specific recognition moiety) for TYR, which was successfully employed in intracellular imaging and tissues using TP microscopy. However, as the demand for early clinical diagnosis expands, there is a growing gap for a rapid, simple to prepare, and highly sensitive technique of TYR detection.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a rising star of zero-dimension carbon-based nanomaterials [26]. CDs exhibit many remarkable advantages as fluorescent probe, such as inexpensive preparation, low cytotoxicity, photo-induced electron transfer ability, stable optical properties, tunable photoluminescence behavior, and chemical diversity [27–29]. In addition, abundant controllable functional groups on CDs surface guarantee positive and extraordinary effects in electrical energy storage and conversion [30]. As a result, CDs have attracted tremendous focus in frontier areas such as bioimaging, biosensor, drug delivery, cancer therapy, and photocatalysis in recent years [31–34]. For instance, alpha-l-fucosidase (AFU) is a common biomarker in hepatocellular carcinoma. Leblanc et al. developed a fluorescent probe to monitor AFU in human serum based on the energy transfer between CDs and gold nanoparticles [35]. The proposed method had a good linear range, which sufficiently cover AFU levels of both healthy and patients with hepatocellular carcinoma.

Here, we optimally synthesized DA-CDs from citric acid (CA) and dopamine (DA) using a one-step microwave-assisted approach. Microwave-assisted strategy is a safe and convenient method, DA-CDs emitted bright fluorescence within 330 s. We confirmed the biosafety of DA-CDs in vivo and in vitro. DA-CDs had good selectivity and sensitivity for TYR with a detection range of 0.2–5 U/mL, and a detection limit of 0.018 U/mL. Finally, DA-CDs were successfully applied to the detection of TYR activity in water samples, cell lysates, and serum of pulmonary metastatic melanoma mice.

Chemical reagents were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Various biological small molecules, proteins, and enzymes were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). All other chemicals and biological reagents were obtained from qualified reagent suppliers without further purification.

The particle size distribution and roughness information of DA-CDs were measured by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL Ltd, Japan, 200 kV) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Bruker, Germany) in tap mode, respectively. Molecular vibration information was collected with a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan, IR Affinity-1). The UV absorption and fluorescence spectra of DA-CDs solutions were scanned by UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan, UV-3600) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan, F-2500), respectively.

A sample of 0.3843 g of CA and 0.1532 g of DA were dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water and then heated with a microwave-assisted approach (700 W) for 330 s. After that, the brownish-yellow product was dissolved in distilled water, and the pH was adjusted to 7.0 with 1.0 M sodium hydroxide solution. After pH adjustment, the DA-CDs solution was filtered (0.22 μm) and dialyzed (24 h) sequentially, and the final solution was stored in a refrigerator at −20 °C.

Because the activity of tyrosine was strongest at around pH = 6.8, we gradually diluted the DA-CDs solution with 0.01 M of PBS buffer (pH = 7.0) until the fluorescence signal reached maximum intensity at maximum excitation. Under the neutral condition, the concentration of DA-CDs after optimization was 40 μg/mL. Then, 1 mL of DA-CDs optimization solution was added to a clean quartz cuvette for measurement. TYR at a concentration gradient of 0–20 U/mL was reacted with DA-CDs for 30 min at 37 °C, respectively. Then, all fluorescence spectra from 375 nm to 625 nm were collected at a maximum excitation of 320 nm. The fluorescence quenching rate (Q) was calculated using the following equation:
Q =
(
F 0
− F )
/
F 0
where F represents the quenching fluorescence of the solution after the addition of TYR, and F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of DA-CDs in 0.01 M of PBS (pH = 7.0). The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the CDs was determined by using fluorescein (QY = 0.79, dissolved in 0.1 M NaOH) as a standard and calculated according to the following equation:
QY x
=
QY r
(
I x
I r
)
(
A r
A x
)
(
η
2
x
η
2
r
)

The subscripts “x” and “r” denote CDs and fluorescein, respectively. I is the integrated emission intensity, A is the absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The light absorption values of CDs and fluorescein solutions are kept below 0.06 to avoid the effect of reabsorption.

All cell lines (HepG2, NIH-3T3, and B16F10) were purchased from American Type Culture Collection and cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (Gibco). The media were supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (HyClone), 100 units/mL penicillin (Sangon), and 100 μg/mL streptomycin (Sangon). Cells were incubated at 37 °C in a humidified incubator containing 5% CO2. In cytotoxicity assay, the cell suspension of HepG2 (5000 cells/well) was seeded in a 96-well plate and cultured overnight. Then, HepG2 was incubated with or without DA-CDs (final concentration: 40 μg/mL) for 24 h, 48 h, and 72 h. Cell viability was measured by Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8); 10 μL of CCK-8 solution (Biosharp) was added to each test well and then the 96-well plate was placed in the incubator for 1 h. The absorbance at 450 nm was read by a microplate reader (BioTek). In clone formation assay, HepG2 was seeded into a six-well plate at a density of 200 cells/well. After HepG2 was cultured overnight, the DA-CDs (final concentration: 40 μg/mL) were pipetted into the test well. PBS group was used as a control. The cells were cultured for 10 days until colonies were clearly visible (>50 cells). All test wells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (Sangon), then stained with 0.02% (w/v) crystal violet (Sigma) for 5 min at room temperature, and counted by a light microscope. Each treatment was performed in triplicate.

All animal experiments in this study followed the guidelines and ethics of the Animal Care and Use Committee of Southeast University (Nanjing, China). Ten-week-old C57BL/6J female mice were purchased from the Changzhou Cavens Laboratory Animal Co. Ltd (China). For the safety assessment, six mice were randomly divided into two treatment groups (PBS and DA-CDs; n = 3). The mice were intravenously injected with 100 μL of 0.01 M of PBS and 100 μL of 40 μg/mL of DA-CDs at the beginning, respectively. The body weight of the mice was monitored daily. Various tissues including the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney were harvested for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) analysis. In the pulmonary metastatic melanoma model, mice were established by intravenous injection of 2 × 105 B16F10 cells (n = 9). Three mice were euthanized every 5 days, and the lung and serum were collected for subsequent analysis, respectively.

A sample of 104 cells (NIH-3T3, HepG2, and B16F10) in the logarithmic growth phase was collected. The 100 μL of cell lysate buffer (Invitrogen) was added to cells and lysed at room temperature for 5 min. After centrifugation, the obtained supernatant was diluted to single-cell lysate with 0.01 M of PBS (pH = 7.0). Finally, DA-CDs (final concentration: 40 μg/mL) were added to single-cell lysate for TYR assay. In another assay, to avoid TYR activity beyond the detection range in fresh mouse serum, mouse serum was diluted 10-fold for subsequent TYR activity detection.

The CA and DA were placed into a flask and then rapidly produce CDs in 330 s by a one-step microwave-assisted method (Fig. 1 ). The UV absorption of carbonized CA and carbonized DA were first tested and shown in Fig. S1. Although carbonized DA had a strong absorption peak at 390 nm, the extremely low solubility of carbonized DA in aqueous solution will weaken the fluorescence (Fig. S1b). The DA-CDs synthesized with different molar ratios of DA and CA (1:3 to 3:1) were shown in Fig. S2. The solubility of DA-CDs decreased gradually in aqueous solution with the increase of DA concentration during carbonization (Fig. S2a). The optical properties were also changed accordingly (Fig. S2b). The products prepared with 3:1 M ratio of CA and DA showed stronger fluorescence intensity than that of 2:1. However, it was not stable because a large amount of black insoluble black aggregates were formed at room temperature in 3 months (Fig. S2b). Thus, a 2:1 M ratio of CA to DA was the most suitable to synthesize DA-CDs. Meanwhile, we explored the relationship between the fluorescence signal of CDs and heating time under this condition. Fig. S2c showed that the solution color turned yellow and darkened with the increase of heating time within 0–360 s. The result implied that the CA and DA gradually carbonized. The strongest fluorescence intensity of CDs was fired at 330 s as shown in Fig. S2d. In summary, we figured out the best route for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (DA-CDs). The synthetic method of our DA-CDs was also compared with other reported methods (Table S1) [36–39]. Obviously, we developed an easier and faster method, which did not require any crosslinking agents and can be used for mass production.

In addition, TEM and AFM were used to characterize the morphology and structure of DA-CDs. Fig. 2 a displayed DA-CDs were sphere-like with uniform size and good dispersion. The particle size of DA-CDs conformed to the Gaussian distribution with an average size of about 5.62 nm (Fig. 2a). Meanwhile, the surface morphology of the DA-CDs was also measured by AFM. The results proved that the height of DA-CDs was between 5 and 6 nm (Fig. 2b). The high-resolution (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2c) further indicates that DA-CDs had a high crystallinity with a lattice spacing of 0.33 nm, which was consistent with the (002) crystal plane of graphite [40]. We next clarify the crystallographic characteristics of DA-CDs by X-ray powder diffractometer. The data demonstrated the broad peak of DA-CDs centered at 24.18° (Fig. S3), which coincided with the result of HRTEM image that DA-CDs had a graphene-like structure (Fig. 2c). FT-IR was employed to explore the functional groups on the surface of DA-CDs (Fig. 2d). The broad and intense absorption bands around 3450 cm−1 corresponded to C–O–H stretching vibration. Remarkably, the FT-IR spectrum exhibited there were C–H, C=O, C=C, and C–O groups on the surface of DA-CDs (Fig. 2d). DA-CDs also displayed fewer absorption peaks in the fingerprint region (1300–500 cm−1) than the uncarbonized CA and DA mixture (Fig. 2d), which was also strong evidence of CDs generation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties, stability, and biosafety research
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the fluorescence properties of DA-CDs, the DA-CDs solution was scanned by the UV–vis and fluorescence spectrometers, respectively. DA-CDs showed a broad UV absorption band at 320 nm (Fig. 3 a), which was attributed to the n-π∗ transitions of the C–OH bond in sp3 hybrid regions at the edge of CDs [41]. It might be the reason for the strong fluorescence intensity emitted by DA-CDs. In the fluorescence spectrum, excitation of 320 nm yielded the maximum emission at 450 nm (Fig. 3a). The fluorescence QY is an important parameter for the optical properties of CDs. The QY of DA-CDs was 10.27%, and the fluorescein was as a reference (Fig. 3b). In addition, the qualitative relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH (pH = 1–14) was investigated (Fig. 3c). Under the neutral condition, the DA-CDs solution emitted bright blue fluorescence. However, as the alkalinity or acidity of the solution increased, the fluorescence intensity of DA-CDs gradually weakened (Fig. 3c). Meanwhile, DA-CDs had the strongest fluorescent signal at a concentration of 40 μg/mL under neutral condition (Fig. S4). Given the pattern of concentration and pH changes, 40 μg/mL of DA-CDs under neutral conditions was used for subsequent experiments. To test the resistance of DA-CDs to photobleaching, the DA-CDs solution was continuously exposed to a 320 nm light for 4 h (Fig. S5). The data indicated that DA-CDs had superior resistance to photobleaching.

The cytotoxicity of DA-CDs was evaluated in vitro by CCK-8 assay and cell clone formation assay, respectively. HepG2 was cultured in 96-well plates for 24–72 h to detect cell viability (Fig. 4 a) and proliferated in six-well plates for up to 10 days to observe the number of cell clones (Fig. 4b and c), respectively. Compared with PBS group, the high cell viability and unchanged cell clone number demonstrated DA-CDs possessed excellent biocompatibility in vitro (Fig. 4a–c). To examine DA-CDs in vivo biosafety, we injected 100 μL of DA-CDs into each mouse via tail vein. The result showed that DA-CDs had a negligible effect on mouse body weight (Fig. S6). H&E-stained sections further demonstrated that DA-CDs had no toxicity and side-effects on the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney in mice (Fig. 4d). The above data indicated DA-CDs with good photobleaching resistance ability, and high biosafety hold biosensing potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective response of TYR and mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: DA-CDs were derived from CA and DA. CA acted as the carbon source, and DA acted as the donor of functional groups on the surface of the DA-CDs. According to the previous reports, DA can be specifically catalyzed and oxidized to o-dopaquinone by TYR [42]. O-dopaquinone was an effective electron acceptor resulting in fluorescence quenching when coupled to a fluorescent substrate [43]. Thus, to test the specificity and selectivity of DA-CDs for TYR, a series of representative substances were investigated in the sensing system. As show in Fig. 5 , leucine (Leu), tryptophan (Trp), cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), N-acetyl-l-cysteine (NAC), glutathione (GSH), glucose (GLU), urea, FBS (fetal bovine serum), bovine serum albumin (BSA), immunoglobulin G (IgG), acid phosphatase (ACP), ALP (alkaline phosphatase), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), glucose oxidase (GOX), lysozyme (LZM), esterase (EST), protein kinase A (PKA), and H2O2 had a negligible effect on the sensing system (Fig. 5). The DAwas also added to the sensing system, it cannot interfere with the fluorescence of DA-CDs. (Fig. S7). Therefore, the fluorescence of DA-CDs was specifically quenched by TYR, which supported the enrichment of DA residues (bisphenolic hydroxy) on the surface of CDs.

When DA residues in DA-CDs conjugate converted into o-dopaquinone (electron acceptor), an efficient photo-induced electron transfer process then took place resulting in fluorescence of DA-CDs (electron donor) quenching. In addition, in the coexistence of TYR, the above series of interfering substances had no obvious effect on the fluorescence of DA-CDs (Fig. S8). Although higher concentrations of biothiols (Hcy, Cys, GSH) may have a little recovery effect on fluorescence, such high concentrations of biothiols were not present in our actual samples and therefore were ignored. To further validate the enzyme-catalyzed oxidation reaction between TYR and DA-CDs, kojic acid (a typical TYR inhibitor) was used in this assay. With the gradual increase of kojic acid concentration, the effect of TYR on fluorescence of DA-CDs was weakened (Fig. S9). It suggested the generation of o-dopaquinone moiety in the surface of DA-CDs was regulated through catalytic oxidation of TYR. The above results indicated that the DA-CDs-based sensing system had good specificity and selectivity toward TYR.

Sensitive detection of TYR activity was carried out under optimal experimental conditions. Fig. 6 a showed a series of solutions after reaction under UV light irradiation. The corresponding solutions were that DA-CDs incubated with various concentration of TYR (the final concentration: 0–20 U/mL) for 30 min, respectively. The fluorescence of DA-CDs gradually weakened with the increase of TYR activity (Fig. 6a and b). It illustrated that TYR caused the fluorescence quenching of DA-CDs in a dose-dependent manner and showed a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.9929) over the concentration range of 0.2–5 U/mL (Fig. 6c). The linear regression equation was (F0–F)/F0 = 0.1959C + 0.03847 (C: TYR concentration), and its detection limit (LOD) was 0.018 U/mL. The LOD was calculated to according to the formula LOD = 3σ/K (σ is the standard deviation of 11 determinations of blank samples, K is the slope between the fluorescence intensity ratio versus analyte concentration) [44]. The linear range and LOD of TYR activity were compared with previous reports, respectively (Table S2) [45–51]. Accordingly, we developed a highly sensitive and selective TYR sensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TYR activity in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the feasibility and accuracy of the proposed sensing system, the detection of TYR activity in tap water sample was conducted using a standard addition method. Three different concentrations of TYR (1, 2, 5 U/mL) within the calibration linear range were added to tap water samples. Afterward, the percentage recoveries were calculated and displayed in Fig. 7 a. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 5.0–7.5%, and the good recoveries were in the range of 96.8–109.3%. The results indicated that the DA-CDs-based sensing system was reliable for the detection of TYR activity in water samples. We then applied the sensing system to cell lysates because TYR was a key biomarker for melanoma. Three cell lines, NIH-3T3 (a TYR-negative normal cell line), HepG2 (a TYR-weakly-positive non-melanoma cancer cell line), and B16F10 (a TYR-strongly-positive melanoma cell line), were selected to evaluate our sensing system. The results showed B16F10 had high TYR activity of 4.2 U/mL, HepG2 had medium TYR activity of 1.8 U/mL, while NIH-3T3 had low TYR activity of 0.7 U/mL in single-cell lysate (Fig. 7b). Inspired by the successful application of our sensing system in water samples and cell lysates, we finally applied it in the pulmonary metastatic melanoma mouse model. Pulmonary metastatic melanoma model was generated on C57BL/6J mice through intravenously injection B16F10 cells. We euthanized three mice every 5 days to observe the metastasis of melanocytes in the lungs and collected serum from the mice. The visualization of representative lungs and HE-stained sections showed the number and volume of melanoma nodules in the lung of mice increased over time (Fig. 7c and d). The results indicated that the number of B16F10 in mice blood was increasing rapidly. Then, the TYR activity of all mouse serums was detected and shown in Fig. 7e. The TYR activity in the mice serums continued to increase from 0 to 10 days, which was consistent with the results of previous lung lesions in the mice (Fig. 7c and d). These data suggested our sensing system had the ability to monitor cancer metastasis in patients with melanoma in real time by capturing TYR in the serum.

In conclusion, we used CA and DA as raw materials for the rapid preparation of DA-CDs by a one-step microwave-assisted method and further optimized the preparation strategy. The prepared DA-CDs had excellent biocompatibility in vivo and in vitro. Due to the photo-induced electron transfer effect, DA-CDs had a good linear range for accurate measurement of TYR activity. Based on DA-CDs, we developed a selective and sensitive strategy for the determination of TYR activity in water samples, cell lysates, and serum samples. Notably, the measurement of TYR activity in pulmonary metastatic melanoma mice model could provide information for the diagnosis of the metastatic process of melanoma.

T.L. conceived and supervised the project. T.L. and Y.C. designed the experiments. Y.C. and Y.W. performed main experiments and analyzed the data. T.L. and Y.C. wrote the manuscript. All the authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX21-0145).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtsust.2022.100207.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The global burden of melanoma: results from the global burden of disease study 2015

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Karimkhani, C.; Green, A. C.; Nijsten, T.; Weinstock, M. A.; Dellavalle, R. P.; Naghavi, M.; Fitzmaurice, C., The global burden of melanoma: results from the Global Burden of Disease Study 2015. Brit. J. Dermatol. 177, 134-140 (2017).
Schadendorf, D.; van Akkooi, A. C. J.; Berking, C.; Griewank, K. G.; Gutzmer, R.; Hauschild, A.; Stang, A.; Roesch, A.; Ugurel, S., Melanoma. Lancet 392, 971-984 (2018).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of Tyrosinase in living cells using an Enteromorpha Prolifera based fluorescent probe

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Dai, Q.; Wang, X. J.; Liu, C.; Feng, Z. Z.; Ge, B. S.; Ma, X. Q.; Zhang, Z. X.; Yu, J. Y.; Wang, X. Q.; Huang, F., Detection of Tyrosinase in living cells using an Enteromorpha Prolifera based fluorescent probe. Anal. Chim. Acta 1169, 338605 (2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal complex as an optical sensing platform for rapid multimodal recognition of a pathogenic biomarker in real-life samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Dey, N.; Biswakarma, D.; Bhattacharya, S., Metal Complex as an Optical Sensing Platform for Rapid Multimodal Recognition of a Pathogenic Biomarker in Real-Life Samples. Acs Sustain. Chem. Eng. 7, 569-577 (2019).
